Round 2 Recap · Weekend Preview

the Memorial Tournament

Muirfield Village Golf Club · Dublin, Ohio · June 4–7, 2026 · Par 72 · ~7,569 yards
Signature event · 72 players · $20M · no cut Firm & dry Wind builds Fri–Sun Defending champ: Scottie Scheffler (back-to-back, 2024–25) Five decades at Jack’s place — 50th anniversary Built from a multi-year venue archive + DataGolf Top-150 + forecast weather

Memorial Tournament — Final

J.T. Poston wins the Memorial, beating Ryan Gerard in a playoff after both finished -12. Wyndham Clark was solo third at -11. It was a ball-striker’s week on a firm, windy Muirfield Village — Poston led the field in Strokes Gained: Total (+16.8 over four rounds) and never let the wind take the trophy.

Champion’s scorecard — J.T. Poston

Round-by-round, with ball-striking and putting SG. The wind ball held up Sunday.

The 60-Second Readthe whole week, before the tables

Muirfield is the Tour’s hardest second-shot test — firm, dry and long: 68% of approaches come from 150+ yards, so long-iron quality and a clean short game decide it, not driving. Scottie Scheffler is the runaway pick (+3.44 SG/round here, back-to-back champ, half a stroke clear in the model). Thursday opens calm and Sunday closes calmest; the separation comes on a windy Saturday and a Friday afternoon that stiffens hard — so the early-AM draw is the edge.

FRI R216builds PM

SUN R46calmest

Scheffler’s SG/rd here — half a stroke clear of the field

of approaches from 150+ yds — a long-iron test
7→16
Thu→Fri toll: want the early draw before PM stiffens
Top picksSchefflerCantlaySchauffeleSi Woo KimFitzpatrickLowryÅberg

The Setup

The 50th Memorial — firm, dry, no-cut. The Tour’s hardest second-shot test, and Scheffler’s house.

Jack Nicklaus’s Muirfield Village hosts the 50th Memorial as a no-cut, 72-player signature event ($20M, 43 of the top 50 in the world) — and it arrives firm and dry. Just 1.16″ of rain fell here on May 27, and the seven days before Thursday are forecast bone-dry, so the post-renovation greens — rebuilt firm-and-fast in 2020 — will be at their teeth-baring best. This is the most demanding second-shot test on the PGA Tour calendar, and the analytics scream one name: Scottie Scheffler, who has won the last two Memorials and posts a barely-believable +3.44 SG/round here since the renovation. Round 1 Thursday is the soft spot — calm and benign — and Sunday closes nearly as gently; the one real test is a windy Saturday, gusting past 30, when the firm course bares its teeth.

Course state
Firm
0.00″ of rain in the 7 days before R1; last measurable rain (1.16″) was eight days out and has long since dried. Fast greens, running fairways.
Venue archetype
2nd shot
SG:Approach drives 42–48% of scoring variance every post-reno year — the clearest iron-play premium on Tour. Bombing barely correlates.
Form favorite
+2.95
Scheffler’s DataGolf true-SG total — a half-stroke clear of the field — on the course he’s won twice running. The cleanest spot on the board.
R1 → R2 swing
7→16
Round-1 playability “toll” averages a calm 7; Friday climbs to ~16 as a 6–16 mph SW wind (gusts to 22) stiffens through the afternoon. The Friday draw now tilts hard to the morning.
  • Four of the five post-renovation champions led the field tee-to-green. Cantlay (2021), Horschel (2022) and Scheffler (2024 & 2025) were each SG:T2G #1; only Denny McCarthy (2023, the field’s #1 putter in a tougher scoring year) broke the pattern. At Muirfield, you ball-strike your way to the trophy.
  • The course defends with its long par 4s and its finish. Holes 1, 10, 17 and 18 all stretch 470–490 yards and rank among the six hardest; the closing trio — par-3 16th, par-4 17th, par-4 18th — is one of the sternest finishes in golf.
  • Scoring lives on the par 5s. The four three-shotters (5, 7, 11, 15) are the only holes that play under par — the 15th surrenders a birdie 40% of the time. Separate yourself there, survive the rest.
  • Firm + benign Thursday = a get-ahead-early week. Thursday plays calm tee-to-dusk and Sunday is nearly as soft — the lone gust day is Saturday. Post a number early and let a windy Saturday do the work on the chasers.

The Nicklaus Renovation — Why We Weight 2021–Present

Why we weight 2021–present: Nicklaus rebuilt all 18 greens firm-and-fast in 2020.

Jack Nicklaus closed his masterpiece in July 2020 (right after the COVID-era double-header) for a ~$10M overhaul, and the “new” Muirfield Village debuted at the 2021 Memorial. All 18 greens were rebuilt with sub-air heating/cooling for firmer, faster surfaces; bunkers were reshaped, tees leveled and lengthened, fairways re-grassed and the irrigation rebuilt — with changes on 16 of 18 holes and a virtually-new 15th. The analysis therefore treats 2021 as the layout cutoff: pin-zone priors and per-hole scoring forecasts use the five post-reno editions, while the older 2015–2020 Tourcast data (still archived) is held back unless explicitly toggled in.

Greens & sub-air

the headline change

Every green rebuilt with a sub-air system, producing the firm, fast, repelling surfaces the pros now describe. This is the single biggest reason approach play dominates — miss the number and the ball won’t hold.

Length & bunkering

~7,569 yards, par 72

Tees lengthened and leveled, bunkers reshaped and repositioned. The long par 4s (1/10/17/18) were stretched to 470–490 yards — a relentless mid-iron-into-firm-greens examination.

Why the cutoff matters

muirfield_village_golf: 2021

Pre-2021 greens were softer/slower and the course ~200 yards shorter. Mixing those scoring patterns into pin-zone and hole forecasts would understate today’s difficulty, so they’re excluded by default.

Conditions & Weather

Calm Thursday, windy Saturday, calmest Sunday — Friday afternoon stiffens, so the early draw wins.

Reality check — calm bookends, one windy Saturday

Thursday opens calm (7.2, bottom-decile here) and — the night-before forecast — Sunday now closes the calmest of all (6.2). Friday has firmed up windier, climbing past the venue median to 16 through the afternoon. The lone heavy test is Saturday: 19, a top-10% day for Muirfield (past its p90), gusting to 28. The wind is a one-day spike, not a weekend grind — but Friday afternoon now bites too, so the draw matters more.

The defining fact is dryness. Open-Meteo logs 0.00″ over the seven days before Thursday, with the last meaningful rain (1.16″) eight days out and since baked off by a week of sun and breeze. Expect a fast, running, fully-defended Muirfield. The arc through the week: a benign, near-calm Round 1 Thursday (toll 7); a warm Friday that stiffens hard through the afternoon (sustained 6–16 mph SW, gusts to 22, toll up to ~20 by mid-afternoon); a windy Saturday — gusts to 28 and a day-long toll near 19, with a slim shower chance (0.04″); then a genuinely calm, warm Sunday (toll 6) as the wind swings north and fades.

calm (<9.5) mild (9.5–17.25) moderate windy (24+) R1 mean 7 — calm all day (Muirfield’s median day tolls 10.7)
Round 2 (Friday) hour-by-hour playability — the SW wind builds hard and the afternoon turns genuinely tough. The 7 AM start (toll ~7) matches Thursday, but it jumps into the “mild” band by 9 AM and peaks near 20 mid-afternoon — nearly Saturday-level, and a brutal AM-vs-PM gap. Friday morning is a real prize.

calm (<9.5) mild (9.5–17.25) moderate windy (24+) R2 mean 11.5 ≈ Muirfield’s median; the afternoon peak (~14) is still only ~p70 — the venue tops out near 31 (2019)
  • Antecedent moisture is essentially nil. A bone-dry tournament week on rebuilt sub-air greens means firm, repelling surfaces from the first tee shot — the opposite of last week’s soaked Colonial. Distance control into the greens is everything.
  • Friday is the swing day — and now the draw tilts. Thursday plays flat-calm whenever you tee off (~6.6 both waves), but Friday builds hard — from ~6.6 at dawn to ~14 by mid-afternoon (the field-wide AM/PM gap is a significant +2.2 toll). Under the standard AM/PM rotation, the Thu-PM / Fri-AM half catches Friday morning before the wind peaks (~6.8 + ~10.4) while Thu-AM / Fri-PM trades a dead-calm Thursday dawn for Friday’s windiest hours (~6.5 + ~12.6) — roughly a one-toll-per-round edge to the Thu-PM / Fri-AM side. No longer a coin flip.
  • Saturday is the gust day — Sunday relents. R3 brings sustained 10–16 mph and gusts to 30 (toll ~19, a top-10% Muirfield day) with only a slim shower chance (~0.02″, 43%). Sunday then calms right down — warm, light north wind, toll back to ~9. The weekend isn’t a storm; it’s one windy day bookended by benign ones — and on bone-dry, firm greens, that Saturday wind is a brutal separator.

Second-Shot Golf — The Course

Widest fairways, tightest greens. 68% of approaches from 150+, where the week is decided.

Muirfield Village’s identity is unambiguous in the data: SG:Approach is the dominant scoring skill, carrying 42–48% of round-to-round variance in every post-reno edition, with the per-hole “driver” metric reading app on most holes. It plays a touch over par (birdie ~18%, bogey-or-worse ~21–23%), defended by a run of 470–490-yard par 4s and a vicious closing stretch. The four par 5s are where the birdies hide — though, as the read below shows, they’re the Tour’s stingiest set, so you earn them. Hit your irons; take your medicine on the long 4s.

Position over power — an accuracy venue

−0.11 · favors accuracy — 5-yr range −0.19 to +0.07 (σ 0.09). needle = 5-yr avg tilt · dots = per-year ’21→’25
long · fwy+0.15
long · miss−0.10
short · fwy+0.00
short · miss−0.27
SG:total per drive style across 14 par-4/5 holes. A long ball in the fairway gains +0.15, but a long miss costs −0.10 — nearly erasing the distance — while short-but-straight is flat (+0.00). Net: distance is worth only +0.14 when you find the short grass, accuracy is worth +0.25 even to the bombers.
Where that ranks across the Tour’s venues

Holding accuracy constant, distance buys almost nothing here: a long hitter who misses the fairway gains essentially nothing on a short hitter who finds it — a drive-style SG tilt of −0.31 against the Tour-average −0.21, the 5th-most accuracy-favoring of 34 venues. Distance is neutralized here; iron play and position decide it — the opposite of the bomber-friendly venues at the far end of the spectrum.

Widest fairways, tightest greens

70% fairways — 8th-widest of 37 — but only 55% greens, the tightest of 37 venues. the entire defense is the second shot

Muirfield inverts the usual difficulty. Its fairways are wide — players find them 70% of the time, 8th-widest of 37 venues (Tour average 58%) — so the tee shot is a relative free swing. The greens are another story: at 55% hit in regulation it’s the hardest green-hitting venue of the 37, a full 12 points below average (67%), and the squeeze is worst on the long holes (par-3 GIR 48%, par-4 51%). Firm, fast, fiercely-defended greens reached with long irons — that, not driving, is the test. You’ll be in the fairway, looking at a green you can’t hold.

The approach menu — mid-irons, few wedges

Where that ranks across the Tour’s venues

Strip out the par 5s and Muirfield’s forced approaches are a mid-iron grind. The average is 165 yards (10th-longest of 34 venues), but the shape is the story: only 3.3% are scoring wedges inside 100 yards — half the Tour average and among the fewest — while the single most-common shot is a long iron of 175–200 yards (27%) and 44% play from 175+ — both above the Tour norm. You almost never get a wedge to spin close; it’s a relentless run of mid- and long-irons into firm, repelling greens. Same verdict as the accuracy spectrum, sharpened: iron quality and position, not wedge touch, decide Muirfield.

The par 5s — relief, but the stingiest on Tour

reach green in 233%Tour 50%
eagle look (on in 2)14%Tour 16%
birdie or better35%Tour 49%
plays vs par-0.21Tour -0.42
Reachability from the 2nd shot and scoring across the four par 5s (5 · 7 · 11 · 15), post-reno. “Reach in 2” = ball within 30 yards of the hole after two shots; “eagle look” = on the green in two.
Where that ranks across the Tour’s venues

The four par 5s are Muirfield’s only real relief — but don’t expect a feast. Just 33% are reached in two (5th-hardest of 34 venues), and they yield only 35% birdies-or-better against a Tour average near 49%. At -0.21 to par they are the hardest par-5 set on Tour — every other venue’s play softer. You rarely bomb it home; the play is lay up, wedge it tight, and earn the four you came for.

Par 3 vs 4 vs 5 — the Tour’s hardest test

+1.28 / round · the hardest test on Tour — the average venue plays -0.97, so Muirfield is ~2.3 strokes harder a round. and every par type ranks tougher than the Tour norm

Muirfield is hard everywhere. Its par 3s are the 3rd-hardest set on Tour, its par 5s the hardest, and its 10 par 4s the single hardest par-4 set of any venue — they alone play +1.45 to par per round, the bulk of the damage. The four par 5s claw back -0.85, but stingily (Tour’s fewest birdies), and the par 3s add another +0.68. Net, the round plays +1.28 while the typical Tour stop gives up -0.97. The tournament is won and lost on the par 4s: ten long, mid-iron second shots into firm greens, with no soft holes to coast on.

Hardest holes

post-reno avg score to par

the scoring opportunities
HParYdsGIR/FW±ParB−Bog+Driver
15par 555466% FW-0.32443%11%APP

14par 435781% FW-0.01624%19%APP

The closing gauntlet

The 16-17-18 finish is the toughest three-hole close the field will face: the par-3 16th (199y, +0.20 to par), the par-4 17th (489y, +0.25) and the par-4 18th (480y, +0.30, the hardest hole on the course). Leads are rarely safe here — Scheffler’s last two wins were forged by being the one player still making pars when it mattered. Note the “driver” skill flips toward arg (scrambling) on 18 and 10: when you miss these greens in the wrong spot, getting up-and-down is what saves the card.

Field & Watch List

The 72-man field — the picks that check every box, the fades to avoid.

The board is read through three lenses: DataGolf current true-SG (form), this season’s Tile (ball-striking floor+ceiling: the sum of a player’s 20th- and 80th-percentile SG:ball-striking — a consistency-and-upside measure) and consistency (mean per-hole SG percentile across 2026), and a player’s post-reno Muirfield record. The names that check all three are the bets; the ones with form but no venue fit are the fades. Every column is heat-mapped to its own range (greenest = field-best); Shot Q blends each player’s 2026 shot profile (80%) with their post-reno Muirfield shot history (20%) into one field-relative percentile. The last column, R1+2 Toll, flags this week’s draw — each player’s average playability toll across their actual Round 1 and Round 2 tee times (lower = the easier two-day rotation, essentially who drew Friday morning).

Column definitions & methodology

DG Tot/APP/OTT/Putt = DataGolf true SG per round (6-month, this week’s field). ’26 Tile = season SG:ball-striking p20+p80. Consist = mean per-hole SG percentile (higher = steadier). Muir SG/rd = post-reno (2021–25) strokes-gained per round at Muirfield. Shot Q = each player’s expected SG/round from their shot distribution (how often they play from each distance/lie bin × their skill there), blended 80% 2026 season + 20% post-reno Muirfield, then ranked vs the field. R1 Tee / R2 Tee = the player’s published Round 1 and Round 2 tee times (all off #1; morning tinted blue, afternoon amber). R1+2 Toll = the average playability ‘toll’ across the player’s actual Round 1 + Round 2 tee times — lower = the easier draw (Thursday plays flat-calm, so the spread is essentially the Friday AM/PM wave). Every numeric column is heat-mapped to its own range (greenest = field-best; for Toll, green = lowest).

All three boxes checked

Elite-or-near-elite approach (the venue’s key skill) + strong 2026 Tile/consistency + a positive post-reno Muirfield ledger. These profiles fit the test from every angle.

Scottie Scheffler ☑☑☑ defending champ
The most lopsided venue edge in the field. Back-to-back Memorial winner (2024 −8, 2025 −10), each time leading the field in both SG:APP and SG:T2G. His post-reno Muirfield line is absurd: +3.44 SG/round, −2.19 to par/round across four starts, +2.42 of it on approach alone. DataGolf has him a full half-stroke clear of the field (+2.95/rd). On a second-shot course, the best iron player alive is the bet to beat.
Patrick Cantlay venue horse · 2021 champ
Won here in 2021 (playoff over Morikawa) leading the field T2G. Quietly the #2 post-reno Muirfield performer behind Scheffler: +1.97 SG/round, −0.61 to par/round over five starts. Approach-leaning game (APP/rd +1.10 here) is the exact profile the venue rewards; the putter is the only swing factor.
Xander Schauffele ☑☑☑
Elite approach (DG SG:APP #14) and a deep, steady Muirfield book — +1.37 SG/round over five post-reno starts, +0.91 of it on irons. The kind of mistake-free ball-striker who racks up pars on the brutal 1/10/17/18 stretch and feasts on the par 5s.
Si Woo Kim ☑☑☑
Under-the-radar triple-box fit: DG SG:APP #13, the field's quietly-strong +1.58 SG/round at post-reno Muirfield (5 starts), and a top-tier 2026 ball-striking Tile (+0.24). Profiles as a sneaky-good outright at a price.
Matt Fitzpatrick elite irons
The field's #4-ranked approach player (DG SG:APP +0.92) with a +0.78 SG/round Muirfield record and a top-five 2026 ball-striking Tile. Precision over power is his whole game — tailor-made for a venue where the irons decide it.
Shane Lowry approach + grind
DG SG:APP #10 and a positive Muirfield ledger (+1.03 SG/round, 5 starts). Thrives in the wind that arrives Friday and lingers through the weekend; the kind of player who shrugs off a tough scoring setup.
Ludvig Åberg ceiling play
The field's #2 approach player (DG SG:APP +1.00) and +2.09 DG total — only Scheffler is clearly better. Just two Muirfield starts but already +1.41 SG/round. If the irons are on, the ceiling is the trophy.

Long-shot edge — the debutant to watch

Alex Fitzpatrick has no post-reno Muirfield record (a Memorial first-timer), but his 2026 ball-striking Tile (+0.30) is the second-best in the entire field behind only McIlroy — an elite-floor-and-ceiling ball-striker walking into a course that rewards exactly that. Among the eight Memorial debutants, he’s the one whose game most clearly projects to travel.

Fade / Caution Signals

  • Kurt Kitayama. Has the skill that should fit — DG SG:APP #9 — but a brutal −3.16 SG/round at post-reno Muirfield across three starts. The course and the player keep disagreeing; fade until it stops.
  • Cameron Young. DG #2 in the entire field by raw skill (+2.35), but a Muirfield record of +2.07 to par/round. Elite tools, no venue results yet — a name to respect for a top-20 but tough to trust outright here.
  • Min Woo Lee. Min Woo's distance is a weapon most weeks, but his approach ranks #83 in the field — the wrong end of the bell curve for the one skill Muirfield demands — and he's +2.5 to par/round here.

The Iron-Bin Edge — Who the Distance Mix Helps

Who’s genuinely good from the long irons this course demands — SG quality, not how often.

Muirfield is a long-iron course: just 6% of approaches come from inside 100 yards (0.72× the Tour rate — the wedge game is starved), while 68% come from 150+ — 37% from the 150–200 band (the single busiest) and another 31% from 200+ (1.1× the Tour rate). So what matters isn’t how often a player faces each distance — that just reflects how far they hit it — it’s how well they score from the irons this course actually asks for. Each cell below is the player’s strokes-gained-per-shot percentile in that band this season (green = field-best quality, not field-most-often); Fit weights those by Muirfield’s distance demand — so the table rewards being genuinely good at the long irons, not just living there.

Who the long-iron demand helps — and exposes

The cleanest fits gain strokes exactly where Muirfield lives. Si Woo Kim tops it — 100th percentile from 150–200 (the busiest band) and 89th from 200+ — and his +1.58/rd over 20 Muirfield rounds backs the read. Adam Scott (97th and 97th in the two long bins) and Rory McIlroy (96th from 150–200, 99th from 200+, and elite from inside 100 at 97th — only his 100–150 is soft) are pure long-iron ball-strikers; Kurt Kitayama (99th from 150–200) and Matt Fitzpatrick (field-best 100th from 200+) own a single elite long bin. Ryan Gerard is the steadiest profile in the field — green in every band (71–94) with no hole — the safest read of the group. These are players whose best irons are the irons this course demands most.

The mix mutes rather than sinks the top names. Scottie Scheffler is elite from the long bins (93rd at 150–200, 83rd from 200+) — his lone hole is 100–150 (6th) — and at +3.44/rd (the field’s best Muirfield history) it’s noise. Cameron Young actually grades a strong iron fit (87th from 150–200); his red flag is venue history (−0.35/rd), not his irons — the classic “due” case. The players it genuinely exposes are the ones whose edge sits where Muirfield won’t hand it to them. Lucas Glover is the textbook case: 99th percentile inside 100 but bottom-tier from every distance that matters (4th / 7th / 24th), so his one elite tool is the 6%-of-the-time shot (−0.71/rd here). Justin Thomas can do everything but the one thing he’ll face most — 3rd percentile from 150–200, the busiest band (−0.54/rd). Russell Henley (mid-iron maestro, 97th from 100–150, but field-worst 1st from 200+) and Chris Gotterup (86th from 100–150, 4th from 200+) do their scoring inside 150 — precisely the range this course de-emphasizes. And read the spread within a row as ceiling-vs-floor: the most polarized profiles — Thomas (3→93) and Glover (4→99) — are boom-or-bust, and Muirfield decides which bin they live in. Aaron Rai, the reigning PGA champion, is a mid-iron specialist (field-best 100th from 100–150, 94th at 150–200) but thin from the longest (31st) and off the wedge fringe (0) — a narrow, well-placed game built for the 100–200 core.

Scrambling on the Tightest Greens

Hardest greens to hit on Tour, so up-and-down carries: who saves par when approaches miss.

The flip side of the hardest greens to hit on Tour (55% GIR): you will miss roughly 8 a round, so the short game isn’t a luxury here — it’s load-bearing. Up-and-down rate (par-or-better when you miss the green) and SG around the green decide whether those misses are pars or bogeys. On greens this firm, a missed approach with no recovery is a straight dropped shot.

The both-ends profile this course demands

The names you want pair ball-striking with recovery. Russell Henley leads the field at 71% up-and-down and owns its best Muirfield record (+1.0/rd); Tommy Fleetwood (99th in SG:ARG, 97th scrambling) and Scottie Scheffler (93rd/96th, plus a top-10 putter) are elite at both ends — exactly the rare combination firm greens reward. The danger sign is the iron-strong but short-game-light trap: Daniel Berger and Aaron Rai graded as long-iron fits a section ago, yet sit in the field’s bottom third around the green — one missed green with no up-and-down and the card leaks. Lucas Glover is the cautionary case: the highest long-iron ceiling in the field undone by 47th-pctile recovery and 8th-pctile putting. A great approach you can’t hold means nothing if the miss isn’t saved.

The Complete Picture — Who Checks Every Box

Every lens stacked into one Muirfield Fit number — who checks every box.

Stack the lenses — and weight them by how Muirfield actually rewards skill, not a generic blend. Approach 50% (SG quality weighted to the course’s 150+-yard-heavy distance mix), short game 38% (around-the-green, weighted toward rough — 54% of greenside shots here start from rough vs 34% Tour-wide), putting 5% and driving 7% (driving scored on accuracy, not distance). Putting is deliberately tiny for a reason: tested against recent form at Muirfield’s comp courses, a player’s going-in putting predicts their putting there (r = +0.61) but says nothing about the rest of their game (r = −0.18) — it’s no signal of course fit. Approach is the strongest honest predictor. Read the Fit against consistency and history here.

Irons (approach, bin-weighted) / Short (around-green, rough-weighted) / Putt / Drv (driving accuracy) = 2026 field percentiles (green = best); Fit = App 50 · Short 38 · Putt 5 · Drv 7 · scroll for all qualified golfers

71Brian Campbell823298347-5.420

The archetype match

Scottie Scheffler is the runaway fit (composite 90): the distance mix mutes one tool, but he ranks top-10 in the field in every other — long irons, short game, putting — with the field’s best consistency and Muirfield record (+3.4/rd). The course punishes a weakness more than it rewards a strength, and he hasn’t got one. The chasing checks-every-box tier pairs a long-iron game with real recovery: Si Woo Kim, Tommy Fleetwood, Hideki Matsuyama and Ludvig Åberg — and, notably, Cameron Young, flagged as iron-bin-disadvantaged earlier but rescued here by elite scrambling and driving into the top five.

The synthesis also tempers the single-section reads. Daniel Berger and Aaron Rai had the irons but, without a short game, slide well down. Lucas Glover’s long-iron ceiling collapses to the bottom of the field once putting and recovery enter. The players who appear in the top tier of every section — Scheffler, Fleetwood, Matsuyama, Henley — are the real archetype matches: their Muirfield edge isn’t one number, it’s the absence of a hole to attack.

Courses That Play Like Muirfield — and the Form That Confirms It

The comp set, and whether players’ recent form there backs the fit (r = +0.56).

Every venue in the 37-course archive gets a fingerprint — green difficulty, fairway width, approach length, par-type difficulty, scrambling difficulty, and the accuracy-vs-distance tilt — standardized and measured against Muirfield’s. The nearest neighbors aren’t a surprise: they’re the Tour’s hard, second-shot, ball-strikers’ courses. The question is whether that comp set actually predicts who plays well here — and the recent form says it does.

Muirfield’s nearest neighbors

closest fingerprints across the 37-venue archive (similarity, 100 = identical profile)
CourseSimilarityWhy it plays the same
TPC Sawgrass
100
the PLAYERS — 60% greens, plays over par, accuracy-defended: the Tour’s other great second-shot test
Quail Hollow
100
Wells Fargo / PGA — the longest mid-iron diet (172y) and a brutal closing run of par 4s
Hurstbourne
92
ISCO — tight 60% greens that play well over par
Caves Valley
91
BMW — a 200-yard approach diet; a pure ball-striker’s course
Colonial
80
Charles Schwab — Hogan’s Alley: position and precision over power
Aronimink
78
PGA Championship — Donald Ross, second-hardest in the set, repelling greens
The Renaissance
77
Genesis Scottish Open — long approaches into a neutral-to-hard, accuracy-first test
Bay Hill
75
Arnold Palmer Inv. — firm, long and demanding into small greens

Does it pan out? — r = +0.56

Pooling each player’s SG-total per round across the eight most-similar courses over the last ~12 months and correlating it with their Muirfield Fit (the composite from the section above) returns r = +0.56 — a strong link. The players our model grades as Muirfield archetypes are, in fact, the ones lighting up Muirfield-like courses right now. Fit also tracks actual Muirfield history at r = +0.40. The archetype read isn’t just theory; it shows up in the results.

Reading the tape

The cleanest confirmations are the players who top all three — fit, comp-course form and Muirfield history. Scottie Scheffler (Fit 90, +2.8/rd at the comps, +3.4/rd at Muirfield) and Ludvig Åberg (the field’s best comp form at +3.2/rd, +1.4 here) are the model working end-to-end; Rory McIlroy, Matt Fitzpatrick and Tommy Fleetwood follow the same pattern. The most interesting name is Cameron Young: a top-five fit with the field’s fifth-best comp form (+2.7/rd), yet a negative Muirfield record — the similar-course data argues his history here undersells the fit, and a breakthrough is overdue. At the other end, the low-fit names (Brian Campbell, Tom Hoge) are also the ones losing strokes at Muirfield-like courses — the comp set flags them just as it flags the contenders.

Player Notebook — From the Press Room

What the field and Jack said this week, tied back to the analysis.

What the host and the field said this week at Muirfield — and how it squares with the analysis above. Verbatim from the Tuesday–Wednesday press conferences.

Jack Nicklaus — the design intent

“I didn’t design it for big hitters, didn’t design it for short hitters, didn’t design it for the middle.” · “I never played with spin. I played with trajectory, not spin… spin gets you in a little bit of trouble.” On the firm greens (3, 6, 9, 10, 14, 15, 17, 18): “If you spin the ball off of those greens… not a good result.” straight from the architect — the bomber-neutral, accuracy-first, firm-green read the data shows

Scottie Scheffler — going for three straight

“The golf course is already firming up pretty good. It doesn’t look like we have much rain in the forecast, so it should be another pretty challenging week.” · “It’s a bit more of a placement course than Bay Hill.” · “At certain holes there’s nowhere to bail out… you just got to step up there and hit the shot.” · On the three-peat: “a cool place to… win the same tournament three years in a row, especially with it being Jack’s Place.” firm + dry + placement — the #1 Muirfield fit, in his own words

Rory McIlroy — back after missing 2025

“The course is as hard as ever.” · “The fairways are very generous… if you miss the fairway, I feel like you deserve a bad lie.” · “The greens are rolling around 11, 11.2… I really don’t think they need to get much faster.” wide fairways, brutal misses, fast greens — exactly the ‘widest fairways / tightest greens’ profile

Aaron Rai — the 2026 PGA champion

“It requires a complete game no matter what your skill set is… It requires everything.” · “One, that it’s really hard… it definitely requires a little bit more understanding.” the ‘who checks every box’ synthesis in a single sentence

Wyndham Clark — in form

“This course you got to be playing from the fairways.” · “When you get out of position at this golf course you got to take your medicine and eliminate the big number.” · “I’ve been hitting it good, really starting to putt it good.” position-first, avoid the blow-up — the par-4 survival test

Round 1 Outlook — Thursday

Thursday’s the soft spot — calm and benign. Go low early.

Round 1 is the week’s scoring opportunity. With light morning winds (toll holds near 6, dipping to ~3.8 around 8 AM) and dry, sunny skies, the early wave gets Muirfield at its most gettable before Friday’s wind. But “gettable” is relative on a firm second-shot course — the long 4s and the closing stretch still demand precise irons, and the par 5s are where the red numbers come from.

  • Where to score: the par 5s — 15th (40% birdie, easiest on the course), 7th (33%), 5th (32%), 11th (29%) — plus the short par-4 14th (24%). A clean Thursday means going at least 3-under on the 5s.
  • Where to survive: the opener (1st, 486y, +0.20), and the 10th/17th/18th. Pars on the long 4s are quietly worth their weight; the field bleeds shots there all day.
  • Draw read: Thursday’s early starters get the calmest stretch of the week and should set the early number — but under the AM/PM rotation they pay it back Friday afternoon in the strongest wind, so a hot Thursday morning isn’t a free tournament-long edge. The breeze freshens gently through Thursday afternoon (toll ~7.5 by the close) and Friday’s is stiffer still.
  • Player to beat from the gun: Scheffler. He has opened sub-70 in each of his Muirfield wins and leads the field in the exact skill (approach) that Thursday’s firm greens reward. Anyone chasing wants to bank birdies on the 5s before he does.

Hole-by-Hole — Muirfield Village

Every hole scored from the five post-renovation Memorials, then overlaid with Friday’s forecast SW wind decomposed against each hole’s calibrated compass bearing. Par 72, ~7,569 yards. The venue’s signature shows up in the per-hole SG breakdowns below — iron play (APP) is the dominant scoring skill on the majority of holes, and raw distance barely moves the needle — the longest hitters gain just +0.04 strokes/round here vs ~+1.0 at the average Tour course, more accuracy-favoring than 95% of venues (see the “Position over power” spectrum on the Preview tab).

Course Difficulty Profile

Hover a hole (tap on mobile) to see its year-by-year scoring and how much it has swung from 2021–25.
  • The defense is the finish and the long par 4s. 18 (+0.30), 17, 10, and the par-3 12th/16th rank hardest; holes 1/10/17/18 all stretch 470–490 yards into firm greens.
  • Scoring lives on the par 5s. 15 (−0.32), 7, 5 and 11 are the only holes under par — the 15th yields a birdie-or-better ~43% of the time.

Per-Hole Historical Profile

The full venue table: scoring and variability, the skill that decides each hole (Driver), whether yardage or pin placement drives the difficulty (Yds fx), and whether length or accuracy is rewarded (Tilt).

Driver (share of scoring variance): OTT APP ARG PUTT PSp = how much the pin placement swings scoring · r(toll) = weather–score correlation Tilt: red = accuracy-favoring / blue = bomber-favoring

Friday’s Wind, Hole by Hole

Decomposing Friday’s ~12 mph southwesterly against each hole’s bearing shows the draw’s teeth: the front-nine turn (7·8·9) and 15 play straight into it, while the closing holes ride downwind. The gold needle on each hole card points tee→green; the blue arrow is the wind.

Into the wind harder Friday
7 · 8 · 9 · 15
Downwind more gettable
1 · 2 · 5 · 13 · 16 · 17 · 18
Crosswind control test
3 · 4 · 6 · 10 · 11 · 12 · 14
  • The turn bites; the closers relax. 7·8·9 (the front-nine turn) and 15 play dead into the breeze, while the closers 16·17·18 ride downwind — the homestretch gets a Friday-afternoon helping hand.
  • The par-5 relief shifts. The 5th rides downwind (even more reachable Friday), while 7 and 15 turn into the breeze and 11 fights a crosswind — the gettable par 5 is the 5th.
  • Most weather-sensitive historically: the par-3 4th (1.9% of scoring variance explained by wind), the 10th (0.8%) and 12th (0.5%) — small everywhere (Muirfield is a stable-conditions venue), but these short-iron holes feel it most.

The 18

Wind-dial guide
Wind dial: the mini hole is its real shape — tee (bottom) → green (top), doglegs and all → blue arrow = wind — down = into, up = helping, sideways = crosswind

Pin Zones & Tendencies

This week’s official hole-locations sheet won’t publish until tournament week, so this is the next best thing: where the PGA actually puts the pins at Muirfield and how each spot scores, clustered from the five post-renovation Memorials (~20 pins per hole). Each shape is the green’s real outline from the official PGA Tour pin-sheet, with every pin from the five post-reno Memorials (2021–25) plotted on it and grouped into numbered pin zones (① = toughest on that green). Clusters are colored by difficulty relative to the other pins on the same hole — green = the easiest spot here, red = the toughest here — so the per-hole spread stands out. The table under each green breaks every zone down — ±par, birdie / bogey-plus rates, and raw strokes-gained by approach, around-the-green and putting — each cell heat-shaded relative to that hole (green = the best zone in the column, red = the worst).

The five toughest pins on the property

18 · front-mid-right+0.36 to par · 9%B · 37%Bg+
12 · back-right+0.35 to par · 7%B · 32%Bg+
1 · back-left+0.29 to par · 6%B · 32%Bg+
17 · mid-left+0.27 to par · 8%B · 31%Bg+
10 · back-right+0.27 to par · 8%B · 31%Bg+

A pattern: the hardest placements are back or short-sided on the long par 4s (18, 10, 1, 17) and the par-3 12th — exactly where a firm, repelling green leaves a brutal up-and-down.

Round 2 — How They Did It

Poston leads at -9 through 36 with the cut made. The forecast firmed up overnight: both waves now play brutal — AM ~15 and PM ~16 Toll, essentially a wash (and the day’s worst gust window is in the morning). The calm-AM edge the chasers were counting on is gone — it comes down to raw skill in the wind, not the draw. Below: who has the best path to win, where the numbers say to buy and sell, and the R3 plays.

Best Round 2 rounds

Who gained the most strokes Saturday-relevant in R2 (heat-mapped vs field).

PlayerR2 SGBall-str.Putt
J. Poston+11.4+6.4+3.5
R. Gerard+9.4+3.1+7.5
S. Burns+7.3+5.8+1.6
T. Fleetwood+5.6-0.1+3.0
E. Cole+5.0+1.3+0.6
W. Clark+4.6+3.6-0.3
P. Cantlay+4.2+4.2+1.4
M. McNealy+4.1-2.3+3.6

Who Can Win — Tournament Opportunities

In control — and the draw is a wash

Top of the board. The forecast flip actually helps them: the morning is no calmer (AM ~15, PM ~16 Toll), so the afternoon draw isn’t the penalty it looked like. Still beatable — these profiles are short on raw skill — but only by someone going low in the wind.

PlayerNowSkillR3 draw
J. Poston1 (-9)+0.40PM 17
R. Gerard2 (-8)+1.31PM 17
S. Burns3 (-6)+1.34PM 17
T. Fleetwood4 (-4)+1.43PM 17

Dark-horse paths — elite hands, no draw help

The most skilled players are chasing, but the morning gives them nothing (~15 Toll, if anything tougher than the PM). Their path is pure ball-striking in the wind — still the best live-outright value, but they have to earn it.

PlayerNowSkillR3 draw
R. McIlroyT19 (+1)+2.22AM 15
M. FitzpatrickT28 (+2)+2.11AM 15
A. ScottT28 (+2)+1.47AM 15

Saturday’s Draw — Both Waves Brutal

AM vs PM conditions

The forecast firmed up overnight — no soft side. AM ~15 and PM ~16 Toll (weak 0.2/1 split), and the day’s worst gust window is in the morning. No draw edge to play.

AM · if anything tougher
Field Toll15.4
PM · brutal too
Field Toll16.1

Player Notebook — From the Press Room

Verbatim from Friday’s press room at Muirfield — and how it squares with the analysis above.

J.T. Poston — leading into the weekend

“The greens are as good as we will see all year. They’re firm, fairways are running… it makes the spots on the greens really, really small.” · “I switched to a different golf ball last week… it’s supposed to help me a little bit in the wind.” · “A day like today is going to give me a lot of confidence going into the weekend.” leads at −9 — and with both waves blowing ~15-16 Toll all day, that wind ball travels right through the weekend

Scottie Scheffler — grinding from the morning wave

“I felt like I was going to shoot about 90 today… I couldn’t imagine I hit more than six or seven greens.” · “I still managed to shoot even par around a golf course that requires you to strike the ball really well.” the buy-low / dark-horse read — elite hands scoring on an off day, and that ball-striking is exactly what travels on a no-edge, ~16-Toll Saturday

Tommy Fleetwood — the test rewards the best

“It was pretty brutal… the greens were crispy.” · “The wind could do anything, and on top of that, it was very, very firm.” · “A good test of golf would be the best player being able to separate himself from somebody that’s not playing anywhere near as good.” our consistency model has Fleetwood elite-and-repeatable; firm + windy is exactly the setup that separates the field

Sam Burns — the margins are razor-thin

“The margins are just so thin of where you have to land the ball… a couple yards here or there and it’s the difference in making birdie and making bogey.” · “Every single shot… it feels like a U.S. Open.” why the variance / buy-sell card matters — at these margins a few bounces and the putter swing scores hard

Justin Thomas — conditions corroboration

“That was the hardest round of golf that I can remember, major, non-major — it was just insane.” · “I’m two good rounds away from being right there.” backs the Toll model’s call that Friday stiffened — and the weekend draw keeps the wind in play

Ryan Gerard — the monster

“Mentally and physically this place is a monster… the rough is thick.” · “I hit it in two hazards off the tee on par-5s on the back nine and hit both greens in regulation from 240-plus.” Gerard is our top ‘sell’ (hottest putter) — scoring that isn’t built on tee-to-green tends to cool

Round 1 Recap · Round 2 PreviewThursday is in the books — Friday’s angles

Four share the lead at −5 — but Fleetwood got there on a hot short game with flat ball-striking (his words: “I got the most out of the round”), while Clark and Nick Taylor struck it elite with cold putters — the more durable hand. Scheffler is buried at T36 after an off day and the worst draw, but the model still has him #1 in skill. Friday tilts late: the AM wave plays ~3 toll easier before the wind firms up.

AnglesFleetwood: which one?Clark / Taylor buyA. Fitzpatrick reboundScheffler @ +790AM draw edge

Round 1 — How They Did It

A four-way logjam at −5 — but the how splits them. The SG breakdown shows who built it on durable ball-striking (BS) vs the regressy putter/short game (Putt/ARG). Field averaged +1.06; defending champ Scheffler sits T36 (+1) after an off ball-striking day and the last-off draw.

The Read — Buy Low / Sell High

Ball-striking is sticky; putting and short game are noisy. Cross R1’s SG decomposition with Friday’s draw and you get the actionable angles.

Tommy Fleetwood▲▼ it cuts both ways · PM draw
His −5 came almost entirely from a red-hot short game — putting +2.9, chipping +3.1 — with ball-striking flat (+0.1). He said it himself: “I got the most out of the round… I could have played great and shot level or 1-over.” So: if he keeps striking it like today, he’s a fade (the short-game heat regresses and he drew Friday’s tougher PM wave). But if you think the ball-striking comes up while the short game stays hot, he runs away with it — that combination wins by five. Your call on which Fleetwood shows up.
Wyndham Clark & Nick Taylor🟪 buy / hold · PM draw
Both −4/−5 on elite ball-striking (Clark BS +3.8, Taylor +4.2) with neutral putters — the sustainable way to do it. Clark: “every time I was in the fairway I gave myself a really good chance.” Scores hold or improve if the flat-stick warms; the only drag is they both drew the PM wind.
Alex Fitzpatrick🟪 deep rebound · AM draw
Struck it as well as anyone in the field — BS +4.1 — but putted it away (−2.1) to a flat even par (T32). Pure positive-regression profile, and he’s got the soft AM draw. The kind of name that jumps 20 spots if the putter is merely average.
Scottie Scheffler🎯 buy at a price · +790
Model still has the #1 skill in the field; one off ball-striking round (−1.1) plus the worst possible last-off draw isn’t a trend. “I felt like I was hitting some good shots… didn’t really get much for it.” T36 and +790 to win is the value angle on the defending champ.
Harris English🔥 sell, with a caveat · AM draw
−1 on a scorching putter (+3.8) that papered over ball-striking −2.2 — that gap usually closes the wrong way. The one thing keeping it from a clean fade: he drew the soft AM wave.

R1 Stat Recap — By the Numbers

Round 1 graded against the two things we set before a ball was struck — the Muirfield fit read and each player’s season baseline — plus who’s due to regress, who’s a rebound, who was most repeatable, and who left it all on the greens.

vs the Pre-Tournament Fit Read

Two ways to read it. Overall the model was directionally right — good fits finished higher (a modest but real +0.23 rank correlation) — but a one-round sample always has defiers. The biggest: Burns (fit #65 → R1 #6) lit it up; Smalley (fit #13 → R1 #72) no-showed.

Friday’s Draw — The Wave Tilts Late

The smart split has the morning (24 players) playing a toll of 11.6 before the wind builds, and the afternoon (48 players) into 15.4 — a 3.8-toll swing (~half a shot). The morning wave got the break.

AM wave toll
11.6
24 players, calmer before the SW wind firms up.
PM wave toll
15.4
48 players into the teeth — gusts to 25+.
Field range
11–16
Per-player projected Toll exposure across the day.

Who drew what (current top 20)

AM (the break): Ryan Gerard, Tony Finau, Alex Noren, Harris English, Taylor Pendrith, Maverick McNealy.
PM (the test): Tommy Fleetwood, J.J. Spaun, Wyndham Clark, Nick Taylor, Justin Rose, Sam Burns, Ryan Fox, Shane Lowry, Patrick Cantlay, J.T. Poston, Rory McIlroy, Keegan Bradley.

Draw-adjusted takeaway: leaders Fleetwood / Clark / Taylor drew the tougher PM, while co-leader Gerard caught the softer AM break — joining English, Finau and A. Fitzpatrick in the quieter window to make a move.

Round-Score Model vs the Book

Par is 72; the book lines Friday tough (72.5–73.5) for the windy afternoon. Our model = season skill (DataGolf SG/rd) adjusted for each player’s draw (AM/PM Toll), calibrated to the market baseline so only real gaps flag as value. Lower = better.

Posted O/U lines — our lean

PlayerWaveLineProjLean
Alex NorenAM72.572.5EVEN
Nicolai HøjgaardAM72.572.5EVEN
Ryan GerardAM73.572.7UNDER
Si Woo KimAM72.572.3UNDER
Harris EnglishAM72.572.8OVER

Gerard at 73.5 is the standout — the book respects his AM draw, but our model still has the −5 man under it.

Hole-by-Hole — Round 2 Forecast

Tomorrow’s official pins + Round 1’s scoring + Friday’s wind, run through the score-forecast model. Course-wide: B− 21% / Par 56% / Bg+ 23%. The par 5s (5/7/11/15) are the scoring holes; 1, 8, 17, 18 are the grind. H9’s back pin guts its birdie rate.

Player Notebook — After Round 1

Verbatim from Thursday’s post-round interviews.

Ryan Gerard −5 (T1)

“I don’t think we aimed at a flag stick on the back nine and shot 5-under.” · “The margin of error is really, really small on these golf courses when greens are firm.” · “The game has been trending for a while. We’re just starting to see the results.”

Tommy Fleetwood −5 (T1)

“I got the most out of the round, totally.” · “I shot 5-under. I could have played great and shot level or 1-over, so, like, take that when it comes.” · “The margins are so small… it just puts such a premium on your ball control.”

Wyndham Clark −5 (T1)

“The golf course is so demanding. I think I made three saves by chipping out and having a hundred yards in.” · “I like when it’s difficult and you have to… get up-and-down and huge par saves.”

J.J. Spaun −5 (T1)

“I thought I did well between the ears today.” · “When you make the turn at 4-under like I did, you’re like, okay, I can really go low.” · “Putting’s feeling good… hopefully it stays hot.”

Nick Taylor −4 (T5)

“Hitting the fairways here is important with how high the rough is. But once you’re coming into the greens, you just got to be precise.” · “It’s just very hard around the greens if you’re in the rough… the greens running away from you.”

Rory McIlroy

“The greens are very firm already, very fast. Get yourself above the hole, it’s very tricky.” · “There’s 69 more holes to go… there’s a long way to go.”

Scottie Scheffler +1 (T36)

“The wind is just hard to predict.” · “All I can do is compete with the hand I’ve been given, and teeing off last on this golf course is challenging.” · “I felt like I was hitting some good shots… didn’t really get much for it.”

Round-2 score O/U (par 72)

Posted lines: Noren O/U 72.5 (−138/+100) · Højgaard 72.5 (−134/−102) · Gerard 73.5 (+112/−150) · Si Woo Kim 72.5 (+114/−152) · English 72.5 (−142/+106). See the model leans above.

Coming: per-hole player odds (1–4)

Drop in the hole-by-hole player prices for holes 1–4 and I’ll add a section cross-referencing them against the forecasted bird/bog rates by wave.

About · methodology & sources

StimpDeck — live PGA tournament analytics. This pre-tournament preview was generated Monday, June 1, 2026, from StimpDeck’s newly-built Muirfield Village venue archive (11 Memorials, 2015–2025, calibrated for hole-aware conditions), the 2026-season player database, the DataGolf Top-150 (Current) true-SG query, and Open-Meteo’s forecast model.

Sources & method: Per-hole difficulty, the venue archetype, post-reno winners’ SG ranks, and Muirfield per-player records are computed by StimpDeck from PGA Tourcast shot data (post-renovation cutoff 2021). Field per thegolfnewsnet.com; the event is a 72-player signature field. Current-form true-SG (DG Tot/APP/OTT/Putt) from DataGolf’s Top-150 (Current) query; season Tile and consistency from StimpDeck’s 2026 baselines (19 events). Hourly playability “toll” and precipitation from Open-Meteo, decomposed against each hole’s compass bearing (hole-1 calibration 76°). Winners and scores cross-checked against the StimpDeck archive.

Generated Mon Jun 1, 2026 · Muirfield Village Golf Club, par 72, ~7,569 yards · figures subject to change as tee times publish and the forecast evolves.
